section.title: The Call of Samuel
section.sectionRationale: In a time when the word of the LORD was rare, God speaks. He calls the boy Samuel by name in the night, three times, until Eli understands and teaches him to answer. The LORD entrusts Samuel with a hard word confirming the doom of Eli's house, and Samuel, though afraid, tells it all. From then on the LORD is with him, letting none of his words fall to the ground, and all Israel knows Samuel is confirmed as a prophet. It is one movement: God raises up His prophet and His word returns to Israel.
section.thesis: When God's word had grown scarce and Israel's lamps were nearly out, the LORD calls a listening boy by name, gives him His word even when it is heavy, and establishes him as a trustworthy prophet, so that the God who had fallen silent begins again to speak and to lead His people.
section.frameNote: Four beats: the scarce word and the sleeping sanctuary, verses 1 to 3; the threefold call and Samuel's answer, verses 4 to 10; the word of judgment on Eli's house, verses 11 to 14; and Samuel telling all and being confirmed as prophet, verses 15 to 21.

units.label: The scarce word and the sleeping sanctuary
units.note: the word of the LORD was rare

verses.text: And the boy Samuel ministered to the LORD before Eli. Now in those days the {{dabar|word of the LORD}} was rare, and visions were scarce.
And at that time Eli, whose eyesight had grown so dim that he could not see, was lying in his room.
Before the lamp of God had gone out, Samuel was lying down in the temple of the LORD, where the ark of God was located.

units.label: The threefold call and Samuel's answer
units.note: Speak, for Your servant is listening

verses.text: Then the LORD called to Samuel, and he answered, “Here I am.”
He ran to Eli and said, “Here I am, for you have called me.” “I did not call,” Eli replied. “Go back and lie down.” So he went and lay down.
Once again the LORD called, “Samuel!” So Samuel got up, went to Eli, and said, “Here I am, for you have called me.” “My son, I did not call,” Eli replied. “Go back and lie down.”
Now Samuel did not yet know the LORD, because the word of the LORD had not yet been revealed to him.
Once again, for the third time, the LORD called to Samuel. He got up, went to Eli, and said, “Here I am, for you have called me.” Then Eli realized that it was the LORD who was calling the boy.
“Go and lie down,” he said to Samuel, “and if He calls you, say, ‘Speak, LORD, for Your servant is listening.’” So Samuel went and lay down in his place.
Then the LORD came and stood there, calling as before, “Samuel! Samuel!” And Samuel answered, “Speak, for Your servant is {{shama|listening}}.”

units.label: The word of judgment on Eli's house
units.note: the ears of all who hear it will tingle

verses.text: Then the LORD said to Samuel, “I am about to do something in Israel at which the ears of all who hear it will tingle.
On that day I will carry out against Eli everything I have spoken about his house, from beginning to end.
I told him that I would judge his house forever for the iniquity of which he knows, because his sons blasphemed God and he did not restrain them.
Therefore I have sworn to the house of Eli, ‘The iniquity of Eli’s house shall never be atoned for by sacrifice or offering.’”

units.label: Samuel confirmed as prophet
units.note: the LORD let none of his words fall to the ground

verses.text: Samuel lay down until the morning; then he opened the doors of the house of the LORD. He was afraid to tell Eli the vision,
but Eli called to him and said, “Samuel, my son.” “Here I am,” answered Samuel.
“What was the message He gave you?” Eli asked. “Do not hide it from me. May God punish you, and ever so severely, if you hide from me anything He said to you.”
So Samuel told him everything and did not hide a thing from him. “He is the LORD,” replied Eli. “Let Him do what is good in His eyes.”
And Samuel grew, and the LORD was with him, and He let none of Samuel’s words fall to the ground.
So all Israel from Dan to Beersheba knew that Samuel was confirmed as a {{navi|prophet}} of the LORD.
And the LORD continued to appear at Shiloh, because there He revealed Himself to Samuel by His word.

dabar.gloss: Word, matter, thing spoken. The chapter opens and closes with the word of the LORD: rare at the start, revealed to Samuel at the end. In the Old Testament God's word is never mere information; it is His living speech that judges, saves, and comes to pass, the very thing Israel had been starved of.

shama.gloss: To hear, listen, obey. Eli teaches Samuel to answer, Your servant is listening, and true hearing in Hebrew always leans toward obeying. Samuel's readiness to hear stands against Eli's sons, who would not listen, and models the posture God seeks from His people, faith that comes by hearing.

navi.gloss: Prophet, one who speaks for God. A prophet does not invent his message but delivers the word entrusted to him. Samuel is confirmed as a navi because the LORD lets none of his words fall to the ground; he becomes the first of the great prophets, the mouth through whom God will raise up king and kingdom.

basic.context: Israel is in a spiritual famine: the word of the LORD is rare, visions are few, and even Eli the priest is going blind, a picture of the whole nation's dimness. Then one night God calls the boy Samuel by name. Samuel does not yet know the LORD's voice and keeps running to Eli, until Eli realizes God is calling and teaches him to answer, Speak, LORD, for Your servant is listening. God gives Samuel a hard first message, the certain judgment of Eli's house. Though afraid, Samuel tells Eli everything, and Eli bows to God's will. From then on God is with Samuel, and all Israel knows he is a true prophet. The question is: will God speak again to a people who have stopped listening? He will, through a boy who listens.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative about how God raised up His prophet, not a template promising everyone a dramatic night-time call. Its enduring truths are that God takes the initiative to speak, that hearing God rightly means being ready to obey even hard words, and that a true prophet is known by words that come true. We should not reduce it to a devotional about listening, though listening is real; it is about God restoring His word to Israel through Samuel.
basic.originalLanguages: The chapter is framed by the dabar, the word of the LORD, rare at the start and revealed to Samuel at the end. Eli teaches the boy to say, Your servant is shama, listening, and in Hebrew to hear is to lean toward obeying. By the close, Samuel is confirmed as a navi, a prophet, because God lets none of his words fall to the ground.
basic.typology: Samuel, the prophet raised up when God's word was scarce and confirmed because his words never fail, points forward to the Prophet greater than Samuel, Jesus, the very Word of God made flesh. The listening servant who receives and faithfully delivers God's word foreshadows the perfect Servant who speaks only what the Father gives Him.
basic.christ: Samuel is the first in the great line of prophets that runs to John the Baptist and finds its end in Christ, the Prophet like Moses whom God promised to raise up, whom we must hear. Where Samuel received the word, Jesus is the Word, God's final and full speech to us. And where Samuel spoke a word of unatonable judgment on Eli's house, Christ becomes the atonement no sacrifice at Shiloh could provide, the mediator of a better word.
basic.god: God is the God who speaks and who takes the initiative; He calls Samuel before Samuel knows Him. He is patient, calling again and again until the boy is ready. He is holy and just, keeping His sworn word of judgment against a house that despised Him. And He is faithful to His people, refusing to leave them without His word, raising up a prophet so that His voice is heard from Dan to Beersheba.
basic.crossReferences: Deuteronomy 18 promises a prophet like Moses whom the people must heed. Hebrews 1 says God has now spoken by His Son. Amos 3 says the Lord does nothing without revealing it to His prophets. And 1 Samuel 2 gives the earlier word against Eli that this chapter confirms.
basic.oneStory: In a silent age God speaks again, raising up Samuel as His prophet. Through Samuel God will anoint Saul and David and carry His purposes toward the promised King. The God who speaks here will keep speaking through the prophets until He speaks at last by His Son, the living Word who saves.

context.literary: This chapter closes the opening section on Samuel's rise and confirms the earlier oracle against Eli, chapter 2, now spoken directly to Samuel. The threefold call builds suspense and shows Samuel's inexperience giving way to obedience. The closing summary, verses 19 to 21, marks a turning point: God's word, rare at the start, now fills the land through His prophet.
context.historical: The lamp of God burned in the sanctuary through the night before going out toward morning, so the scene is set in the last hours of darkness, a fitting image for a dim spiritual age. Prophets were God's authorized spokesmen; a prophet's authenticity was tested by whether his words came true, Deuteronomy 18. Dan to Beersheba was a common way of naming the whole land, north to south.
context.canonical: Samuel stands at the head of the prophetic office in Israel, later called the first of the prophets. The chapter develops the Deuteronomy 18 hope of a true prophet whom the people must hear, a hope that runs through the whole prophetic line and is fulfilled in Christ, to whom the Father says, listen to Him.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show God restoring His word to a silent Israel by calling and confirming Samuel as a trustworthy prophet, and to demonstrate that true hearing means readiness to receive and deliver even God's hard words.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes a unique prophetic call; it does not prescribe that every believer will hear an audible night-time voice. What endures is that God takes the initiative to speak, that we should answer with a listening, obedient heart, and that God's word can be trusted because it comes to pass.

debates.question: Why does God repeat the judgment on Eli's house already spoken in chapter 2?

views.label: To confirm and personally commission it through Samuel
views.case: The word first came by an unnamed man of God; now it is given directly to Samuel, establishing him as the prophet who carries God's word and confirming the certainty of the judgment by a second, weightier witness.

views.label: To underline the finality of the sentence
views.case: The added oath, that Eli's iniquity will never be atoned for by sacrifice, intensifies the earlier word, stressing that persistent contempt for God, unrestrained, has passed beyond ordinary remedy.

debates.bearing: Either way the repetition marks Samuel's call as the restoration of God's reliable word and shows that God's warnings are sure, driving the reader to seek the atonement that only Christ can provide.

debates.question: What does it mean that Eli's iniquity will never be atoned for by sacrifice, verse 14?

views.label: A sentence of temporal judgment on the house
views.case: The oath concerns the removal of Eli's priestly line from its office and privilege in history; no ritual offering will avert the fall of the house, though it does not pronounce on the eternal state of every individual in it.

views.label: A warning about presuming on ritual while despising God
views.case: The very sacrifices Eli's sons defiled cannot now cover their sin; the verse exposes the emptiness of religion offered by hearts that hold God in contempt, pointing beyond ritual to the need for real repentance and a better atonement.

debates.bearing: Both readings warn against trusting religious ritual while despising God, and heighten the longing for the true and final atonement in Christ, which alone cleanses what no offering at Shiloh could.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter is bracketed by the dabar, the word of the LORD: at the opening it is rare, and visions are scarce, a famine not of bread but of God's speech; at the close the LORD reveals Himself to Samuel by His word, and it fills the land. In the Old Testament the dabar is never mere information but God's living, effective speech that judges and saves and always comes to pass. At the center Eli teaches the boy to answer, Speak, LORD, for Your servant is shama, listening; and in Hebrew to hear always tilts toward obeying, so Samuel's listening is already the opposite of Eli's sons, who would not listen. By the end Samuel is confirmed as a navi, a prophet, one who does not invent a message but delivers the word entrusted to him, and he is known to be true because the LORD lets none of his words fall to the ground. The God who had fallen silent is speaking again, through a boy who listens.

typology.type: Samuel, the prophet raised up when God's word was scarce
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the Prophet like Moses whom God promised to raise up and whom we must hear, Deuteronomy 18:15-18 and Acts 3:22-23.

typology.type: The listening servant who receives and faithfully delivers God's word
typology.fulfillment: Jesus, the perfect Servant who speaks only what the Father gives Him, John 12:49-50.

typology.type: The word of judgment no sacrifice at Shiloh could atone
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the one true atonement that cleanses what the old offerings could not, Hebrews 10:1-14.

study.christ: Samuel stands at the head of the great line of prophets that runs through Israel's history to John the Baptist and comes to its end in Jesus. Moses had promised that God would raise up a prophet like himself, one the people must heed on pain of judgment, Deuteronomy 18; Peter says that prophet is Christ, Acts 3:22-23. Where Samuel received the word of the LORD, Jesus is the Word of God made flesh, God's full and final speech to us, Hebrews 1:1-2. And where Samuel had to carry a word of judgment on Eli's house that no sacrifice could atone, Christ Himself becomes the atonement that no offering at Shiloh could accomplish, cleansing sin once for all. The boy who answered, Your servant is listening, points us to the Son to whom the Father says, listen to Him.
study.god: God is here revealed as the God who speaks and who moves first: He calls Samuel by name before the boy even knows Him, so that the relationship begins entirely at God's initiative. He is patient, calling again and again through the night until Samuel is ready to hear. He is holy and just, confirming and even intensifying His sworn word of judgment against a house that has despised Him, and He keeps that word to the letter. Yet He is faithful and merciful to His people as a whole, refusing to leave them in the silence they had earned, raising up a prophet through whom His voice sounds again from Dan to Beersheba. This is a God who will be heard, and whose every word stands.

crossReferences.note: God promises a prophet like Moses whom the people must heed, the office Samuel enters and Christ fulfills.
God who spoke through the prophets has in these last days spoken by His Son, the living Word.
The Lord God does nothing without revealing His plan to His servants the prophets, as He does to Samuel.
The earlier oracle against Eli's house that this chapter confirms and delivers directly to Samuel.

study.oneStory: In a silent, dim age when God's word had grown rare, the LORD speaks again by calling a listening boy. Through Samuel God restores His word to Israel and prepares to raise up king and kingdom, anointing Saul and then David on the way to the promised King. The God who begins to speak here will keep speaking through the prophets until, at last, He speaks by His Son, the living Word who accomplishes the atonement and saves His people.

quiz.q: What was the spiritual condition of Israel at the start of the chapter?

options.text: The word of the LORD was rare and visions were scarce, a famine of God's speech.
options.note: Verse 1 sets the scene of spiritual silence that God is about to break.

options.text: Eli's eyesight had grown dim and he could barely see.
options.note: True, and a fitting picture of the age, but the broader point is the scarcity of God's word.

options.text: Israel was enjoying a golden age of prophecy and revelation.
options.note: The opposite; revelation was rare, which is why Samuel's call matters.

options.text: The nation had completely forgotten that God existed.
options.note: They still worshiped at Shiloh; the problem was God's word had grown rare, not total ignorance.

quiz.q: What did Eli teach Samuel to say when God called again?

options.text: Speak, LORD, for Your servant is listening, a posture of ready obedience.
options.note: Verse 9; true hearing in Scripture leans toward obeying.

options.text: To answer the voice rather than keep running to Eli.
options.note: True in substance, but the exact words of humble, obedient listening are the point.

options.text: To demand that God prove who He was first.
options.note: Eli taught humble listening, not a demand for proof.

options.text: To ignore the voice until morning.
options.note: The opposite; Eli taught Samuel to answer and listen.

quiz.q: How is Samuel shown to be a true prophet?

options.text: The LORD was with him and let none of his words fall to the ground, so all Israel recognized him.
options.note: Verses 19 to 20; a true prophet's words come to pass, Deuteronomy 18.

options.text: He faithfully told Eli the whole hard message without hiding anything.
options.note: True and admirable, but the confirming mark is that his words all came true.

options.text: He performed many miracles before the people.
options.note: The text stresses reliable words, not a display of miracles.

options.text: He crowned himself king over Israel.
options.note: Samuel was a prophet and judge, never king.

quiz.q: How does Samuel's call point forward to Christ?

options.text: Samuel heads the line of prophets that ends in Christ, the Prophet like Moses and the Word of God we must hear.
options.note: Deuteronomy 18 and Acts 3 identify that prophet as Jesus, God's final Word.

options.text: He is a faithful servant who delivers only the word God gives him.
options.note: True, and like Christ, but it omits that Jesus is the Prophet and Word Samuel anticipates.

options.text: He shows that God speaks only to children.
options.note: God's initiative to a boy is striking, but the point is not that God speaks only to children.

options.text: He has no connection to Christ.
options.note: Samuel begins the prophetic line fulfilled in Jesus.

quiz.q: What does the chapter reveal about God?

options.text: He takes the initiative to speak, patiently calling, and He keeps both His word of judgment and His faithfulness to His people.
options.note: God calls Samuel first, calls repeatedly, confirms His judgment, and restores His word to Israel.

options.text: He is a God who speaks and reveals Himself to His servants.
options.note: True, but only part of the fuller picture of His initiative, patience, justice, and faithfulness.

options.text: He speaks only through visible signs and never by His word.
options.note: The chapter centers precisely on God speaking by His word.

options.text: He had abandoned Israel and no longer cared to speak.
options.note: The whole point is that God breaks the silence and speaks again.

questions: God's word had grown rare in Israel, yet He spoke to a boy who was ready to listen. Is your life quiet enough, and your heart ready enough, to hear from God through His word?
Samuel answered, Speak, LORD, for Your servant is listening. What would change if you came to Scripture and prayer each day with that same posture of ready obedience?
Samuel was afraid to tell Eli the hard message but told him everything. Where might God be calling you to speak a difficult but true word in love, rather than hiding it?
Eli responded to a hard word with, He is the LORD; let Him do what is good in His eyes. How can you learn to submit to God's will even when it is painful?
God let none of Samuel's words fall to the ground. How does the trustworthiness of God's word give you confidence to build your life on it?

translationNotes.note: Before the lamp of God had gone out places the scene in the last of the night; the sanctuary lamp burned until morning, a fitting image for a dim spiritual age.
His sons blasphemed God follows the Septuagint; the Hebrew reads made themselves contemptible, but the sense of grave, unrestrained sin stands either way.
From Dan to Beersheba is a set expression for the whole land, from its northern to its southern edge, marking that all Israel recognized Samuel as a prophet.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 1 Samuel 3:1-21. Prose narrative. No em or en dashes present in the source or introduced anywhere in the file. Sermon status none: no sermon from the trusted cluster verified this session for this passage.
